name: Creatine Kinase (CK)
summary: Creatine kinase is an enzyme found mainly in skeletal muscle, heart, and brain that helps cells generate energy. Although it is a muscle marker rather than a liver enzyme, it is grouped here because it is routinely used to determine whether an AST/transaminase elevation is coming from muscle rather than the liver.
whatItMeans: An elevated CK most often indicates skeletal-muscle injury or stress, ranging from intense exercise to rhabdomyolysis, statin-associated myopathy, or muscle disease; the cardiac fraction (CK-MB) reflects heart muscle. When AST is high but ALT and CK help show the source, a raised CK with a high AST and normal ALT points to a muscle origin rather than liver injury. Very high CK (thousands U/L) with dark urine raises concern for rhabdomyolysis. This is educational information, not a diagnosis.
raises: Muscle trauma, crush injury, or intramuscular injections
lowers: Resolution of muscle injury or removal of the offending drug
confounders: Intense exercise and creatine supplementation raise CK and can mildly raise AST
Higher muscle mass and male sex raise baseline CK
Black individuals tend to have higher reference CK values than other groups
Recent intramuscular injection or muscle trauma can transiently elevate CK
Hemolyzed sample can affect the result

ranges.note: General adult reference range; varies widely by lab, sex, muscle mass, and ancestry.
Typical adult male reference range (higher due to muscle mass).

references.title: What Are the Normal Serum Creatine Kinase Values for Skeletal Muscle? A Worldwide Systematic Review (PMC)
